anchor.snippet: orthogonality is the generalization of the geometric notion of perpendicularity to linear algebra of bilinear forms
Two elements u and v of a vector space with bilinear form
families of functions are used to form an orthogonal basis
given a pair of conjugate hyperbolas , two conjugate diameters are hyperbolically orthogonal

annotations.label: Orthogonality of eigenstates with distinct eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: a sufficient (but not necessary) condition that two eigenstates of a Hermitian operator
An instruction set is said to be orthogonal if it lacks redundancy
multiple access schemes are orthogonal when an ideal receiver can completely reject arbitrarily strong unwanted signals
One such scheme is time-division multiple access (TDMA), where the orthogonal basis functions are nonoverlapping rectangular pulses
Another scheme is orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM), which refers to the use, by a single transmitter, of a set of frequency multiplexed signals
independent variables that affect a particular dependent variable are said to be orthogonal if they are uncorrelated
an orthogonal classification is one in which no item is a member of more than one group
an orthogonal interaction occurs when there are two pairs of substances and each substance can interact with their respective partner
DNA has two orthogonal pairs: cytosine and guanine form a base-pair
orthogonal protection is a strategy allowing the deprotection of functional groups independently of each other
The term bioorthogonal chemistry refers to any chemical reaction that can occur inside of living systems without interfering with native biochemical processes
the notion of orthogonality refers to the possibility of two or more supramolecular, often non-covalent , interactions being compatible
analyses are "orthogonal" if they make a measurement or identification in completely different ways
orthogonal redundancy is that form of redundancy where the form of backup device or method is completely different
a sensory map in the brain which has overlapping stimulus coding (e.g. location and quality) is called an orthogonal map
'orthogonal' is used to mean "in the same row/'rank' or column/'file'"
